sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Hepatic resection and liver transplantation are potentially curative treatment options for patients with early-stage HCC who meet specific criteria. They are the preferred treatments for patients who meet resection and/or transplant criteria [HCC-4, MS-41].

Principles:
- Patients must be medically fit for a major operation and evaluated by an MDT [HCC-F].
- All patients should be evaluated for possible transplant candidacy [HCC-F].
- Resection requires adequate liver function (generally CTP A, no portal hypertension), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, and adequate future liver remnant (≥20% without cirrhosis, ≥30-40% with CTP A cirrhosis) [HCC-F].
- Transplantation follows UNOS criteria (single tumor ≥2 cm ≤5 cm, or 2-3 tumors ≥1 cm ≤3 cm; AFP ≤1000 ng/mL; no macrovascular invasion; no extrahepatic disease) or expanded/downstaged criteria [HCC-4, HCC-F].
- Minimally invasive approaches by experienced surgeons are safe and effective [MS-14].

Resectability Criteria:
Hepatic resection is indicated for: adequate liver function (CTP Class A without portal hypertension; limited resection in selected CTP B patients), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, adequate future liver remnant (≥20% no cirrhosis, ≥30-40% CTP A cirrhosis), and suitable tumor location. Resection is controversial but can be considered for limited multifocal disease or major vascular invasion in highly selected cases [HCC-F]. Clinically significant portal hypertension (varices, splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia) is a relative contraindication, though its impact may be less if liver synthetic function is preserved [HCC-F, MS-14].

Pre-operative Optimisation:
For patients with chronic liver disease being considered for major resection, preoperative portal vein embolization (PVE) should be considered to induce hypertrophy of the future liver remnant and atrophy of the portion to be resected [HCC-F]. Other investigational methods to improve FLR growth include lobar Y-90 or PVE combined with hepatic vein embolization or arterial embolization [MS-15]. Evaluation of the future liver remnant volume and function is critical.

cards.name: Partial Hepatectomy
cards.detail:
Approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic by experienced surgeons. Involves removal of the tumor-bearing portion of the liver.
Indications: Potentially resectable HCC in patients with CTP A liver function (or highly selected CTP B without portal hypertension), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, and adequate future liver remnant [HCC-F].
Key Complications: Liver failure (especially in cirrhosis), bleeding, bile leak, infection. Postoperative clinical decompensation risk increases with preoperative portal hypertension [MS-14].
Operative Mortality: Low (≤5%) in well-selected patients at experienced centers [MS-14].

cards.name: Liver Transplantation
cards.detail:
Approach: Cadaveric or living donor orthotopic liver transplantation.
Indications: Patients meeting UNOS criteria (as above) or downstaged to within criteria. Treats both the tumor and underlying cirrhosis. Preferred for patients with CTP B or C cirrhosis who are not surgical candidates [HCC-4, HCC-F].
Key Complications: Surgical complications, risks of chronic immunosuppression, infection, renal dysfunction, malignancy. Pre-transplant mortality while waiting on list [MS-18].
Operative Mortality: Variable; cited as a risk but specific rate not provided in source.
